themes.name: Biodiversity Conservation
themes.description: Canada’s 2030 Nature Strategy provides a roadmap to halt and reverse biodiversity loss through a "whole-of-society" approach. It emphasizes shifting from isolated protection to integrated, resilient landscapes by conserving 30% of lands and waters and restoring 30% of degraded ecosystems by 2030. On-the-ground implementation prioritizes Indigenous-led conservation and nature-based solutions, such as wetland restoration and broader ecological connectivity networks that link existing protected areas with remaining critical wildlife habitat. Embedding these solutions into local land-use planning enhances ecosystem health and resilience for the benefit both wildlife and people.

subthemes.name: Ecosystem Resilience
subthemes.description: The ability of an ecosystem to withstand the direct and indirect impacts of natural or human-made disturbances by resisting damage and recovering without losing its core functions and structure. A key characteristic of a resilient ecosystem is high biodiversity, which enables it to adapt more effectively to change. Monitoring changes in adaptive capacity helps identify and prioritize areas in need of habitat protection and restoration.

subthemes.name: Ecosystem Risk
subthemes.description: Environmental risk is the potential for ecosystem collapse and biodiversity loss arising from the cumulative impacts of natural and human disturbances, as well as habitat shifts driven by climate change. This includes both the direct physical impacts of habitat loss and fragmentation on specific ecosystems and species caused by urban development, resource extraction, and outdoor recreation activities, as well as the impacts of natural processes such as wildfires, invasive species, and pest infestations.  It also includes the indirect downstream consequences of these events, which undermine overall ecosystem health and the capacities needed to withstand and recover from ongoing landscape disturbances and climate change.

themes.name: Ecosystem Integrity
themes.description: Terrestrial ecosystems are dynamic and constantly changing. Natural habitats disrupted by human activity and/or altered by changing environmental conditions will recover, depending on the intensity and frequency of landscape disturbances. Terrestrial ecosystems that become highly fragmented and/or sustain ongoing environmental degradation can collapse in areas where adaptive capacity to respond and recover is exceeded. Assessment criteria include levels of habitat connectivity (i.e., lack of fragmentation) and relative degrees of habitat suitability (i.e., biotic value) for representative terrestrial ecosystems at a given location.

subthemes.name: Habitat Values
subthemes.description: An ecosystem's overall condition of health in relation to a natural or relatively undisturbed reference state. It is a measure of the ecosystem's capacity to maintain its biodiversity, ecological functions, and to cope with and adapt to environmental stressors over time. Losses in ecosystem health help identify areas in need of restoration. Gains in ecosystem health over time help identify areas that contribute to biodiversity and require protection.

subthemes.name: Habitat Threats
subthemes.description: Factors or conditions that can cause harm and negatively impact the health and resilience of terrestrial and freshwater habitats. These include human activities that directly disturb the landscape (e.g., urbanization, infrastructure development, agricultural land use, and resource extraction), as well as natural hazard events like floods and wildfires. The cumulative effects of these processes lead to habitat loss and fragmentation, decreasing the natural ability of ecosystems and wildlife species to cope with and adapt to changes over time.

subthemes.name: Habitat Loss
subthemes.description: Habitat loss and fragmentation are two related but distinct concepts in ecology that describe changes in the natural environment caused by the cumulative effects of landscape disturbance and climate change. Habitat loss refers to the permanent destruction, removal, or temporary degradation of terrestrial and freshwater ecosystems. Habitat fragmentation, however, involves breaking larger continuous habitats into smaller, isolated patches. Both habitat loss and fragmentation disrupt natural patterns of ecological connectivity and are the main causes of biodiversity loss.

themes.name: In Situ Biodiversity
themes.description: Measuring biodiversity “in place” involves a combination of direct observation and analytical techniques that assess characteristics of richness (number of speceis), abundance (number of individuals) how evenly these individuals are distributed across the landscape (evenness), and their vulnerability to population declines or local extinction (species risk). Our assessment of in-situ biodiversity for the southern Coast Mountains is based on a curated collection of than 130,000 research-grade observations of plants and animals, and a regional compilation of the ecological communities they are aprt of.

subthemes.name: Forest Ecosystems
subthemes.description: BC’s Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Classification (BEC) classifies broad forested landscapes and site-level vegetated areas based on similarities in climate, geography, and dominant plant and animal communities. Each regional zone reflects long-term interactions between climate and biology that shape the development of distinct habitats, ecological niches, and unique assemblages of plants and animals. In landscape ecology, they help organize the landscape into manageable areas for conservation planning and for understanding how climate change may shift ecosystems at both regional and global scales.

subthemes.name: Ecological Communities
subthemes.description: The Site Level component of BC’s Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Classification (BEC) is defined by unique combinations of understory vegetation, soil characteristics (e.g., moisture and nutrient content), and physiographic features that influence the structure and composition of ecological communities during successional growth stages following episodes of landscape disturbance. Identifying and mapping the distribution of site-level vegetation is vital for practical conservation planning, as most on-the-ground management decisions, like tree species selection for reforestation or defining specific habitat management strategies, happen at this level

subthemes.name: Species-at-Risk
subthemes.description: Species-at-risk (SARs) are native plants and animals in B.C. or Canada that are threatened with extinction due to landscape disturbances that affect ecosystem health and adaptive capacity. Assessments follow NatureServe and COSEWIC standards, ranking species by relative threat. Key factors include total population size (abundance and trends), geographic range and habitat quality (distribution), and exposure to existing or potential environmental stressors. Risk levels range from extirpated or critically imperilled (Red List), highly vulnerable and of special concern (Blue List), to relatively stable with no immediate threats (Yellow List).

themes.name: Climate Change Refugia
themes.description: Areas of the landscape that remain buffered from the most severe effects of climate change, enabling sensitive species to survive locally. They are characterized by stable microclimates, unique hydrological features, or protective topography that provides sanctuary. In the context of biodiversity and landscape ecology, refugia are vital for species survival, acting as "arks" that safeguard genetic diversity and ecological communities. They serve as source populations for future re-colonization of the surrounding landscape, thereby supporting ecosystem resilience and function.

subthemes.name: Climate Refugia
subthemes.description: Areas of the landscape that remain buffered from the most severe effects of climate change, enabling sensitive species to survive locally. They are characterized by stable microclimates, unique hydrological features, or protective topography that provides sanctuary. In the context of biodiversity and landscape ecology, refugia are vital for species survival, acting as "arks" that safeguard genetic diversity and ecological communities. They serve as source populations for future re-colonization of the surrounding landscape, thereby supporting ecosystem resilience and function.

themes.name: Connectivity Network
themes.description: An integrated system of core habitats (protected areas and other undisturbed natural regions) connected by wildlife corridors that are established through coordinated land-use policies to enable the unimpeded movement of species and the flow of natural processes. They are essential for maintaining and restoring ecological functions, halting current trends in biodiversity loss, and helping species adapt to changing environmental conditions in fragmented wildland habitats affected by landscape disturbance and climate change.

subthemes.name: Climate Refugia
subthemes.description: Areas of the landscape that remain buffered from the most severe effects of climate change, enabling sensitive species to survive locally. They are characterized by stable microclimates, unique hydrological features, or protective topography that provides sanctuary. In the context of biodiversity and landscape ecology, refugia are vital for species survival, acting as "arks" that safeguard genetic diversity and ecological communities. They serve as source populations for future re-colonization of the surrounding landscape, thereby supporting ecosystem resilience and function.

subthemes.name: Priority Linkages
subthemes.description: Modelled pathways of ecological connectivity that provide the most efficient movement corridors for wildlife and gene flow across the widest range of species. These pathways identify areas of least resistance for animals and plants to move between protected areas and other high-value habitats, including core and stepping-stone patches found in fragmented landscapes at lower elevations and in valley bottoms. Mapping these movement corridors helps guide the development of practical conservation solutions that deliver the greatest benefits for vulnerable plants and animals.

subthemes.name: Wildland Explorers
subthemes.description: Regional connectivity networks ensure landscape permeability for wide-ranging species that require vast territories to thrive. In the southern Coast Mountains, these include vulnerable populations of grizzly bears, wolverines, and Canada lynx that travel long distances within and between home ranges in search of food and mates. Grizzly bears are used as an "umbrella species" for assessing regional-scale connectivity. Where grizzlies can move safely, it is highly likely that other native species can as well. Focal species in this guild are highly sensitive to human presence. Highway 99 and many of the more popular forest service roads act as barriers to movement across valley bottoms, fracturing and isolating populations that once roamed the entire region.

subthemes.name: Forest & Alpine Specialists
subthemes.description: Ecological connectivity networks at the watershed scale preserve vital linkages for species moving between forest habitats, from the valley bottom to higher elevations. In the rugged South Coast terrain, these include the Pacific fisher, black bear, mountain goat, hoary marmot, coyote, black-tailed deer, yellow-pine chipmunk, and Douglas’s squirrel. These specialists serve as indicators of mature, high-canopy coniferous forest health and rugged escape terrain, where movement is dictated by "stealth and slope" rather than distance. Conservation focuses on protecting remaining mature forest patches and restoring travel corridors to connect isolated populations.

subthemes.name: Valley Dwellers & Commuters
subthemes.description: Fine-scale connectivity is vital for low-mobility, semi-aquatic species such as beavers, mink, toads, frogs, and salamanders, which rely on riparian and neighbouring forest habitats for survival and on reliable water flow for movement. For these animals, the landscape is a series of "hydrologic corridors" where a dry ridge or a disconnected drainage can permanently halt population expansion. Conservation for this guild requires a "watershed-scale" approach that prioritizes restoring riparian corridors and tolerating beaver activity on the landscape. Protecting the beaver ensures the functional connectivity of the entire valley floor, keeping the landscape "wet and connected" for the region's most sensitive residents.

01-01-01-adaptive-capacity.title: Adaptive Capacity
01-01-01-adaptive-capacity.description: Understanding the adaptive capacity of mountain ecosystems is valuable for assessing areas of the landscape most vulnerable to the cumulative impacts of historic and future disturbances, which could lead to habitat fragmentation and the loss of functional ecosystem services we all depend on for survival. Our assessment of ecosystem resilience builds on the IUCN’s Red List of Ecosystems (RLe). It is based on measured decadal changes in ecosystem health (1985 to present) and on projected forest ecosystem turnover due to climatic shifts over the next 50 years.

01-01-02-habitat-protection.title: Habitat Protection
01-01-02-habitat-protection.description: Habitat protection is a proactive approach to safeguarding existing natural environments from harm, thereby maintaining ecosystem health and connectivity. It is a proactive approach, emphasized in the Global Biodiversity Framework's Target 3, which calls for protecting at least 30% of land and seas by 2030 to prevent the loss of biodiversity and ecosystem services. Actions that can be taken at local and regional scales include the formal designation of protected areas managed through legislation or Indigenous law, and the designation of Other Effective Conservation Measures (OECMs)

01-01-03-habitat-restoration.title: Habitat Restoration
01-01-03-habitat-restoration.description: Habitat restoration is the purposeful process of assisting the recovery of degraded, damaged, or destroyed wildland habitats to improve ecosystem health and connectivity and enhance in-situ biodiversity. It is a reactive approach, highlighted by the Global Biodiversity Framework's Target 2, which aims to reverse damage and rebuild resilient landscapes that provide functional ecosystem services. Actions that can be taken at local and regional scales include reforestation of native trees and understory vegetation, eradicating invasive species, and reintroducing native plants and animals.

01-01-04-ecosystem-services.title: Ecosystem Services
01-01-04-ecosystem-services.description: A subjective evaluation of the benefits that current and future generations receive from physical, chemical, and biological processes supporting the functional health and long-term resilience of a natural ecosystem. These include provisioning (food and water), regulating (climate control and disease mitigation), supporting (nutrient cycles and pollination), and cultural services (spiritual and aesthetic value). While our assessment of ecosystem services measures the relative benefits of ecosystem functions in terms of economic value, we acknowledge that all living things have inherent rights and are not to be treated as commodities valued solely for human utility.

01-01-05-enduring-features.title: Enduring Features
01-01-05-enduring-features.description: Enduring features shape the distribution and composition of terrestrial and inland water ecosystems through landscape characteristics like topography, geology, and hydrology, which influence erosion, soil formation, and nutrient cycling. High geodiversity creates microclimates that act as climate refugia, buffering species from temperature extremes and changing precipitation. Conserving a variety of these ensures long-term ecosystem health, providing safe refugia that enable species to redistribute and persist as the climate changes.

01-01-06-land-cover.title: Land Cover
01-01-06-land-cover.description: Visible terrestrial components of the Earth's surface, which include forests, grasslands, wetlands, alpine areas and various land uses associated with human settlement, such as urban and agricultural areas and linear infrastructure. The spatial arrangement and distribution of these components serve as indicators for monitoring landscape patterns and understanding their effects on ecosystem health and integrity over time, and identifying the underlying causes of biodiversity loss.

01-02-01-human-footprint.title: Human Footprint
01-02-01-human-footprint.description: A composite indicator that measures the extent and degree to which human impacts reduce or damage ecosystem health and fragment natural wildland habitats. This includes both the permanent loss of wildland habitat for urban expansion (e.g., environment, roads, energy pipelines, and utility corridors) and shorter-term landscape disturbances caused by agriculture, timber harvesting, and mining. The cumulative impact of increased human activities in these areas results in biodiversity loss and disrupts natural patterns of ecological connectivity.

01-02-02-habitat-fragmentation.title: Habitat Fragmentation
01-02-02-habitat-fragmentation.description: Landsat false-colour imagery visualizes non-visible light—primarily Near-Infrared (NIR) and Shortwave-Infrared (SWIR)—by mapping these bands to the RGB display channels. This process highlights physical properties invisible to the naked eye. In ecology, it is essential for measuring landscape disturbance: healthy vegetation reflects NIR strongly (appearing bright red), while cleared land, roads, or fire scars reflect SWIR and visible light differently. By comparing these spectral signatures over time, researchers precisely quantify habitat fragmentation, forest loss, and the encroachment of human infrastructure into vital ecosystems.

01-02-03-biodiversity-hotspots.title: Biodiversity Hotspots
01-02-03-biodiversity-hotspots.description: Regions with anomalously high concentrations of endemic species and ecological communities that are undergoing significant habitat fragmentation and loss of ecosystem health. Hotspots are ranked based on the relative sensitivity of specific ecological communities to environmental stressors and on the uniqueness and rarity of species assemblages that occur within a given area.  This multi-layered approach measures the "irreplaceability" of a landscape, allowing conservationists to prioritize habitats where small-scale disturbances could trigger disproportionate, irreversible losses to regional biodiversity.

02-01-01-habitat-suitability.title: Habitat Suitability
02-01-01-habitat-suitability.description: The capacity of terrestrial and freshwater ecosystems to provide essential life-cycle resources (food, water, shelter, breeding sites) and suitable living conditions for a species to survive and reproduce. The suitability of a given wildlife habitat is determined by site-specific environmental variables that influence the occurrence and distribution of wildlife species. These include a wide range of biotic and abiotic variables, including the structure and composition of ecological communities within a given biogeoclimatic zone, soil moisture and nutrient levels, topographic position, temperature, slope aspect and solar insolation.

02-01-02-landscape-permeability.title: Landscape Permeability
02-01-02-landscape-permeability.description: The physical arrangement and connectivity of habitat patches and natural landscape features (e.g., forests, water bodies) across a landscape, regardless of species-specific behaviour. Structural connectivity is crucial for biodiversity conservation because it offers essential physical pathways that allow wildlife to move freely to access food, shelter, and breeding sites. This movement supports genetic exchange, enhances ecosystem resilience to disturbances, enables species to adjust their ranges in response to climate change, and diminishes the negative effects of habitat fragmentation

02-02-01-urbanization.title: Urbanization
02-02-01-urbanization.description: The conversion of natural wildland habitats into built environments to accommodate population growth and development. Examples include clearing land for residential neighbourhoods and businesses, agricultural use, resource extraction activities, and associated linear infrastructure such as roads, pipelines, and utility corridors. Direct physical consequences include the loss and fragmentation of wildland habitats and the disruption of natural ecological connectivity pathways. Indirect effects involve altered hydrology (e.g., stormwater runoff) and increased vulnerability to the spread of invasive species and biodiversity loss.

02-02-02-linear-infrastructure.title: Linear Infrastructure
02-02-02-linear-infrastructure.description: Linear infrastructure features include major and minor road networks that connect settled areas, forest service roads that access backcountry areas used for resource development, railways, and cleared rights-of-way used to maintain power transmission lines and utility pipelines.  Each of these features disrupts the natural flow of processes, habitat use, and wildlife movement across the landscape. Zones of influence are measured based on the potential for direct mortality, behavioural avoidance, and movement barriers for different species.

02-02-03-outdoor-recreation.title: Outdoor Recreation
02-02-03-outdoor-recreation.description: Non-motorized recreation is often viewed as benign, but ecological evidence suggests otherwise. High-use hiking and mountain biking trails create a "landscape of fear" for sensitive species. Animals like the Wolverine and Mountain Goat may perceive humans on trails as predators. This triggers a physiological stress response and flight behaviour, often at high energetic cost. If trail use is high enough, it can lead to the functional abandonment of critical habitat, effectively severing connectivity to these life-sustaining areas.

02-02-04-forest-harvest.title: Forest Harvest
02-02-04-forest-harvest.description: While land disturbances caused by timber harvesting will regenerate over time, there are direct and indirect consequences that can be lasting if sustainable management practices are not in place. Direct physical impacts include the loss and fragmentation of wildland habitat, species displacement, alterations in surface and groundwater flow patterns, and disruptions to ecological connectivity networks. Indirect impacts include increased susceptibility to soil compaction and erosion, modifications to natural wildlife behaviour caused by greater access for recreational activities, and the increased spread of invasive species.

02-02-05-invasive-species.title: Invasive Species
02-02-05-invasive-species.description: Invasive species are non-native organisms introduced to new environments that can quickly establish and spread, displacing native plants and animals. Their impact on biodiversity loss includes outcompeting native species for resources, disrupting food webs, and spreading diseases, often causing population declines and extinctions. This loss of native species reduces genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity, which lowers the resilience of terrestrial ecosystems to stressors like climate change by simplifying community structures and impairing key ecological functions.

02-02-06-wildfire.title: Wildfire
02-02-06-wildfire.description: A wildfire conflagration is a severe fire event triggered by a combination of fuel (vegetation), weather conditions (such as high temperatures, strong winds, and drought), and topography (slopes). Wildfires can result in loss of life, destruction of natural habitats, and disruption of ecological connectivity networks. Although some ecosystems are adapted to fire, the increased frequency and intensity of fires can cause lasting changes in vegetation—such as forests turning into shrublands—leading to a decline in the ecosystem's overall health, diversity, and resilience.
02-02-06-wildfire.source: BC Wildfire Provincial Strategic Threat Analysis (PSTA-2025)

02-03-01-forest-ecosystems.title: Forest Ecosystems
02-03-01-forest-ecosystems.description: Loss and fragmentation of forest ecosystems, primarily due to timber harvesting, agriculture, urbanization and wildfire, are critical indicators of habitat disturbance. Removal of tree cover and understory vegetation eliminates homes, food sources, and breeding grounds for many species, leading to habitat fragmentation. The remaining patches of forest cover become isolated, thereby disrupting natural patterns of wildlife movement and the flow of ecological processes. Fragmentation also decreases the amount of suitable habitat along forest edges and increases vulnerability to the impacts of human activity and invasive species.

02-03-02-riparian-and-wetland-areas.title: Riparian and Wetland Areas
02-03-02-riparian-and-wetland-areas.description: Riparian and wetland habitats (vegetation zones along rivers and streams) are highly vulnerable and critical ecosystems that provide essential ecological services and support ecological connectivity networks for plants and animals. They are hotspots of biodiversity and support the life-cycle needs for a majority of freshwater and terrestrial species. Degradation and loss of these habitats severely impact nutrient cycles, the provision of food and clean water, the regulation of natural flood cycles and the buffering of climate change impacts. Their loss destroys critical habitats for fish, amphibians, birds, and mammals, threatening species extinction.

02-03-03-mesic-vegetation.title: Mesic Vegetation
02-03-03-mesic-vegetation.description: Plant communities that thrive in soils with higher moisture and nutrient content in areas of groundwater discharge. These include herbs and shrubs that occur in non-forested areas at mid- and lower-slope elevations and along valley bottoms. Fragmentation and loss of these habitats may lead to instability in the flow and provision of ecosystem functions (e.g., pollination, food provisioning), which are essential to a wide range of terrestrial species. It may also weaken an ecosystem's ability to cope with and adapt to disturbances caused by human activity and climate change.

02-03-04-xeric-vegetation.title: Xeric Vegetation
02-03-04-xeric-vegetation.description: Plant communities that thrive in soils with lower moisture and nutrient levels. These include shrublands in non-forested areas exposed to more sunlight and in drier biogeoclimatic zones of the eastern Coast Mountains. These habitats support species that are uniquely adapted to extreme climatic conditions. Fragmentation and loss of these habitats may cause severe disruption of ecosystem functions, desertification, and reduce the capacity to cope with and adapt to disturbances caused by human activity and climate change.

03-01-01-biogeoclimatic-zones.title: Biogeoclimatic Zones
03-01-01-biogeoclimatic-zones.description: BC’s Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Classification (BEC) classifies broad forested landscapes and site-level vegetated areas based on similarities in climate, geography, and dominant plant and animal communities. Each regional zone reflects long-term interactions between climate and biology that shape the development of distinct habitats, ecological niches, and unique assemblages of plants and animals. In landscape ecology, they help organize the landscape into manageable areas for conservation planning and for understanding how climate change may shift ecosystems at both regional and global scales.

03-02-01-site-series.title: Site Series
03-02-01-site-series.description: The Site Level component of BC’s Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Classification (BEC) is defined by unique combinations of understory vegetation, soil characteristics (e.g., moisture and nutrient content), and physiographic features that influence the structure and composition of ecological communities during successional growth stages following episodes of landscape disturbance. Identifying and mapping the distribution of site-level vegetation is vital for practical conservation planning, as most on-the-ground management decisions, like tree species selection for reforestation or defining specific habitat management strategies, happen at this level

03-03-01-vascular-plants.title: Vascular Plants
03-03-01-vascular-plants.description: Vascular plants (Tracheophyta) have specialized tissues for transporting water and nutrients, allowing them to grow taller and more complex than non-vascular plants. They possess true roots, stems, and leaves. Types include trees, shrubs, grasses, flowering plants (angiosperms), and ferns, many of which produce seeds. Their ability to grow larger and access resources (sunlight, water, nutrients) gives them a competitive advantage, making them the dominant group in most terrestrial ecosystems. There are 60 distinct vascular plant species documented in the southwest Coast Mountains.

03-03-02-mammals.title: Mammals
03-03-02-mammals.description: Vertebrate animals of the class Mammalia are distinguished by having milk-producing mammary glands to nourish their young, a large neocortex region in the brain, body fur or hair for insulation, and three middle ear bones that amplify sound and greatly enhance hearing sensitivity. They are endothermic (warm-blooded), breathe air, and are quadrupedal, with most mammals using four limbs for land movement. More than 80 unique species are known to inhabit the southwest Coast Mountains, ranging in size from small ground squirrels to large-ranging grizzly bears

03-03-03-herpetofauna.title: Herpetofauna
03-03-03-herpetofauna.description: Herptiles are an informal group of vertebrates that includes both amphibians and reptiles found in both terrestrial and freshwater environments. Amphibians (Class Amphibia) are commonly characterized by a life cycle that features an aquatic larval stage with gills and a terrestrial adult stage with lungs. Reptiles (Class Reptilia) are air-breathing, ectothermic (cold-blooded) vertebrates with scaly skin, most of which lay eggs on land. Over 25 distinct species are documented in the southwest Coast Mountains, including various toads, frogs, salamanders, turtles, lizards, and snakes.

04-01-01-climate-refugia.title: Climate Refugia
04-01-01-climate-refugia.description: Areas of the landscape that remain buffered from the most severe effects of climate change, enabling sensitive species to survive locally. They are characterized by stable microclimates, unique hydrological features, or protective topography that provides sanctuary. In the context of biodiversity and landscape ecology, refugia are vital for species survival, acting as "arks" that safeguard genetic diversity and ecological communities. They serve as source populations for future re-colonization of the surrounding landscape, thereby supporting ecosystem resilience and function.

04-01-02-climate-corridors.title: Climate Corridors
04-01-02-climate-corridors.description: Defined geographical areas of natural habitat, riparian zones (areas along rivers or streams), or mountain ranges, that connect larger patches of suitable wildland habitat. They are used to develop conservation strategies to mitigate the effects of a warming climate by providing physical routes for plants and animals to migrate to more suitable, cooler environments (e.g., higher latitudes or elevations). They are a practical, on-the-ground approach to reducing habitat fragmentation and encouraging species dispersal, aiming to build landscape resilience in the southern Coast Mountains.

04-01-03-climate-adaptation-priorities.title: Climate Adaptation Priorities
04-01-03-climate-adaptation-priorities.description: Changes in climate suitability pose a threat to the long-term survival of ecosystems and biodiversity in British Columbia, emphasizing the importance of targeted, climate-aware conservation efforts. Areas that remain relatively undisturbed, indicated by low human footprint, are good candidates for protection and delaying harvesting activities. Conversely, locations affected by human activity but with high potential for climate refugia and connectivity are ideal candidates for ecological restoration, such as wetlands.

05-01-01-climate-refugia.title: Climate Refugia
05-01-01-climate-refugia.description: Areas of the landscape that remain buffered from the most severe effects of climate change, enabling sensitive species to survive locally. They are characterized by stable microclimates, unique hydrological features, or protective topography that provides sanctuary. In the context of biodiversity and landscape ecology, refugia are vital for species survival, acting as "arks" that safeguard genetic diversity and ecological communities. They serve as source populations for future re-colonization of the surrounding landscape, thereby supporting ecosystem resilience and function.

05-02-01-priority-linkages.title: Priority Linkages
05-02-01-priority-linkages.description: Modelled pathways of ecological connectivity that provide the most efficient movement corridors for wildlife and gene flow across the widest range of species. These pathways identify areas of least resistance for animals and plants to move between protected areas and other high-value habitats, including core and stepping-stone patches found in fragmented landscapes at lower elevations and in valley bottoms. Mapping these movement corridors helps guide the development of practical conservation solutions that deliver the greatest benefits for vulnerable plants and animals.
05-02-01-priority-linkages.unit_of_measure: composite cores and multi-species connectivity potential (0-100)

05-03-01-grizzly-bear.title: Grizzly Bear
05-03-01-grizzly-bear.description: Grizzly bears (Ursus arctos horribilis) are powerful omnivores with a distinctive shoulder hump, long claws adapted for digging, and fur that ranges from dark brown to blonde, giving them a “grizzled” appearance. Found in remote mountainous and forested regions of British Columbia, grizzlies are solitary and wide-ranging, with home ranges spanning hundreds of square kilometres. They typically feed on seasonal plants and berries, migrating from low-elevation riparian and valley bottom habitats in the spring to high-elevation alpine meadows and avalanche tracks in the summer. In the fall, they often return to lower elevation riparian networks to feast on salmon and other fish. Winter denning occurs primarily in remote alpine or subalpine environments.
05-03-01-grizzly-bear.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-03-02-wolverine.title: Wolverine
05-03-02-wolverine.description: Wolverines (Gulo gulo luscus) are muscular, solitary carnivores known for their strength, endurance, and tenacity. With thick dark fur, pale facial masks, and bushy tails, they resemble small bears more than weasels. Wolverines are adapted to cold, snowy environments and can travel vast distances in search of food. They scavenge on large carcasses and hunt small prey. In BC, wolverines are rare and elusive, with populations concentrated in remote mountainous terrain. Their low reproductive rate and sensitivity to disturbance make them vulnerable to human disturbances and habitat fragmentation.
05-03-02-wolverine.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-03-03-canada-lynx.title: Canada Lynx
05-03-03-canada-lynx.description: Canada lynx (Lynx canadensis) are elusive, long-legged felines with tufted ears, short tails, and thick fur adapted to cold climates. Their oversized paws act like snowshoes, allowing them to hunt effectively in deep snow. Lynx are highly specialized predators of snowshoe hares, and their populations fluctuate in tandem with hare abundance. Solitary and nocturnal, lynx prefer dense coniferous forests with abundant understory and minimal human disturbance. In BC, they are most common in interior plateaus and northern ranges, with scattered individuals in the Sea-to-Sky region.
05-03-03-canada-lynx.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-01-pacific-fisher.title: Pacific Fisher
05-04-01-pacific-fisher.description: The Columbian population of the Pacific fisher in southwest British Columbia (Pekania pennanti, pop 5) is a secretive, forest-dwelling carnivore in the weasel family. With sleek chocolate-brown fur, bushy tails, and agile bodies, fishers are expert climbers and hunters. They prefer mature coniferous forests with complex canopy structure and abundant windfall timber, which they use for denning and hunting. Despite their name, fishers prey on squirrels, snowshoe hares, and even porcupines. In BC, Pacific fishers are rare and declining, with fragmented populations in the southern interior and isolated individuals in the Sea-to-Sky region.
05-04-01-pacific-fisher.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-02-black-bear.title: Black Bear
05-04-02-black-bear.description: Black bears (Ursus americanus) are adaptable mammals found throughout British Columbia’s diverse landscapes, from coastal rainforests to alpine slopes. They have a stocky build, short claws, and a keen sense of smell that helps them forage for berries, insects, and fish. Their coat can range in colour from jet black to cinnamon or even creamy white. They play a vital role in maintaining healthy ecosystems by dispersing seeds and recycling nutrients. Unless food is abundant, black bears typically hibernate from November to March. From September, bears enter hyperphagia, during which their calorie intake increases from 5,000 to 20,000 per day.
05-04-02-black-bear.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-03-mountain-goat.title: Mountain Goat
05-04-03-mountain-goat.description: Mountain goats (Ursus americanus) are iconic alpine specialists, adapted to life on steep cliffs and rugged terrain. With thick white coats, black horns, and muscular builds, they navigate vertical landscapes with ease. Their split hooves and rough pads provide grip on rock faces, allowing them to escape predators and access high-elevation forage. Found throughout BC’s mountainous regions, mountain goats are sensitive to disturbance and require unobstructed escape terrain. Their seasonal movements follow snowlines and forage availability, often descending in winter and ascending in summer.

05-04-04-hoary-marmot.title: Hoary Marmot
05-04-04-hoary-marmot.description: Hoary marmots (Marmota caligata) are large, social ground squirrels known for their loud whistles, which gave Whistler its name, and communal sunbathing. Their thick silver-grey fur and white muzzle give them a frosted appearance, hence the name “hoary.” Marmots live in colonies and dig extensive burrow systems in alpine meadows, where they forage during the short summer season. They hibernate for up to eight months, relying on fat reserves built during peak forage. In BC, hoary marmots are common in high-elevation habitats, including the Garibaldi and Tantalus ranges.
05-04-04-hoary-marmot.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-05-black-tailed-deer.title: Black-Tailed Deer
05-04-05-black-tailed-deer.description: Black-tailed deer (Odocoileus hemionus columbianus), also known as mule deer, are browsers adapted to BC’s coastal rainforests. With reddish-brown summer coats and darker winter pelage, they’re named for their distinctive black-tipped tails. These deer are crepuscular, feeding at dawn and dusk, and rely on dense understory for cover. Males grow forked antlers annually, shedding them in late winter. Black-tailed deer are a vital prey species for cougars and wolves and play a key role in shaping forest vegetation through browsing.
05-04-05-black-tailed-deer.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-06-coyote.title: Coyote
05-04-06-coyote.description: Coyotes (Canis latrans) are versatile canids distinguished by their pointed ears, slender muzzle, and bushy, black-tipped tail often held downward while running. They have a grizzled, gray-brown coat that changes with the seasons. Highly adaptable, they thrive in diverse habitats ranging from forests and grasslands to dense urban environments. As a focal species, they function as mesopredators, helping regulate small-mammal populations and serving as generalist indicators of ecosystem connectivity and adaptability in fragmented landscapes.
05-04-06-coyote.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-07-douglass-squirrel.title: Douglas's Squirrel
05-04-07-douglass-squirrel.description: The Douglas’s squirrel (Tamiasciurus douglasii) is a lively, vocal tree squirrel with a rusty brown back, pale belly, bushy tail, and a distinct black side stripe in summer. Known for its rapid movements and sharp chatter, it plays a key role in forest ecosystems by dispersing seeds and spores. These squirrels are active year-round, caching cones and mushrooms around the forest. They prefer mature coniferous forests, especially those dominated by Douglas-fir, hemlock, and pine. Their nests, called dreys, are built in tree cavities or branch forks and lined with moss and bark.
05-04-07-douglass-squirrel.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-04-08-yellow-pine-chipmunk.title: Yellow-Pine Chipmunk
05-04-08-yellow-pine-chipmunk.description: The Yellow-pine chipmunk (Neotamias amoenus) is a small, agile rodent with bold facial stripes and a reddish-brown back. Chipmunks are excellent climbers and often forage in shrubs and low trees. They’re active during the day, storing food in cheek pouches and caching seeds for winter. Yellow-pine chipmunks hibernate in colder months, emerging in spring to breed and forage. Their vocalizations include high-pitched chips and trills used for alarm and territory defence.
05-04-08-yellow-pine-chipmunk.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-01-beaver.title: Beaver
05-05-01-beaver.description: The beaver (Castor canadensis) is North America’s largest rodent, easily recognized by its large orange incisors, webbed hind feet, and broad, flat, scaly tail. These semi-aquatic mammals prefer freshwater habitats, such as rivers, streams, and lakes, bordered by abundant trees. Beavers are the quintessential ecosystem engineers and a keystone species; their dam-building fundamentally alters hydrology, creating wetlands that filter water, mitigate droughts, and provide critical habitat for a vast array of other species.
05-05-01-beaver.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-02-mink.title: Mink
05-05-02-mink.description: The American mink (Neovison vison) is a sleek, semi-aquatic weasel characterized by a long, tubular body, dark chocolate-brown fur, and a distinctive white chin patch. They inhabit riparian zones, favouring banks of streams, lakes, and marshes with dense cover. As a focal species, the mink serves as an essential bioindicator of wetland health and water quality. As top-level aquatic predators, they are susceptible to pollutants such as mercury, reflecting the overall condition of their aquatic food webs.
05-05-02-mink.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-03-northern-red-legged-frog.title: Northern Red-Legged Frog
05-05-03-northern-red-legged-frog.description: Northern red-legged frogs (Rana aurora) are slender, long-legged amphibians with smooth skin and a reddish hue on their hind limbs. Their eyes are positioned slightly forward, and they often have a faint gold wash on their back. These frogs breed early in the year, sometimes while snow still lingers, laying egg masses in cool, still wetlands. After metamorphosis, juveniles disperse into moist forest habitats, where they forage and overwinter under logs or in root channels. They are sensitive to habitat disturbance and require cool, shaded environments to thrive.
05-05-03-northern-red-legged-frog.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-04-western-toad.title: Western Toad
05-05-04-western-toad.description: Western toads (Anaxyrus boreas) are stout amphibians with dry, bumpy skin and a pale stripe running down their back. Unlike frogs, they walk rather than hop, and their parotoid glands behind the eyes secrete mild toxins to deter predators. These toads breed in shallow wetlands and lay long strings of eggs. After metamorphosis, juveniles disperse into upland habitats, often traveling hundreds of meters from breeding sites. Western toads are active from spring to fall and hibernate underground during winter.
05-05-04-western-toad.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-05-pacific-tree-frog.title: Pacific Tree Frog
05-05-05-pacific-tree-frog.description: The Pacific Tree frog (Pseudacris regilla) is a tiny, vocal amphibian known for its loud nighttime calls and remarkable colour variation, ranging from green to brown, with a dark eye stripe. They are technically tree frogs and possess toe pads for climbing. These frogs breed in shallow wetlands and temporary pools, laying eggs in clusters attached to vegetation. After metamorphosis, juveniles disperse into upland habitats. Pacific chorus frogs are active from spring through fall and hibernate in leaf litter or underground during winter.
05-05-05-pacific-tree-frog.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-06-ensatina-salamander.title: Ensatina Salamander
05-05-06-ensatina-salamander.description: The Ensatina salamander (Ensatina eschscholtzii) is a small, lungless amphibian with smooth skin and a constricted tail base. Its coloration varies from reddish-brown to orange or gray, often with mottled patterns. Unlike many amphibians, Ensatinas do not have an aquatic larval stage, they hatch directly from eggs laid in moist terrestrial microhabitats. These salamanders breathe through their skin and require cool, damp environments to survive. They are nocturnal and secretive, sheltering under logs, leaf litter, and rocks during the day.
05-05-06-ensatina-salamander.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-07-northwestern-salamander.title: Northwestern Salamander
05-05-07-northwestern-salamander.description: The Northwestern salamander (Ambystoma gracile) is a secretive amphibian with smooth brown skin and large parotoid glands behind the eyes. Adults are terrestrial, while larvae and juvenile individuals remain aquatic. They breed in still or slow-moving water, laying egg masses attached to submerged vegetation. After metamorphosis, juveniles disperse into moist forest habitats, often sheltering under logs, leaf litter, or in burrows. These salamanders are long-lived and sensitive to habitat disturbance, especially changes in moisture and temperature.
05-05-07-northwestern-salamander.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-08-long-toed-salamander.title: Long-Toed Salamander
05-05-08-long-toed-salamander.description: The long-toed salamander (Ambystoma macrodactylum) is a slender amphibian with a dark body, yellow or olive dorsal stripe, and notably elongated fourth toe on the hind foot. It breeds in shallow ponds and lakes, laying eggs singly or in small clusters attached to submerged vegetation. Larvae are aquatic, while adults are terrestrial and nocturnal, sheltering under logs, rocks, and leaf litter. These salamanders are active in spring and fall, often migrating short distances between breeding and foraging habitats. Their permeable skin makes them highly sensitive to pollutants and moisture loss.
05-05-08-long-toed-salamander.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-09-northern-alligator-lizard.title: Northern Alligator Lizard
05-05-09-northern-alligator-lizard.description: Alligator lizards (Elgaria coerulea) are slender, long-bodied reptiles with rough scales and a distinctive fold along each side of the body. Their coloration ranges from olive to brown, with dark bands, and they can drop their tails to escape predators. These lizards are secretive and often found under logs, rocks, or leaf litter. They are active during warm months and hibernate in winter. Alligator lizards give birth to live young rather than laying eggs, a rare trait among reptiles in BC.
05-05-09-northern-alligator-lizard.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential

05-05-10-garter-snake.title: Garter Snake
05-05-10-garter-snake.description: Garter snakes (Thamnophis sirtalis) are slender, non-venomous reptiles with distinctive longitudinal stripes, typically yellow, green, or blue, running down their dark bodies. Males are considerably smaller than females. They are highly adaptable and often found near water, where they hunt amphibians and small fish. Garter snakes are active during the day and hibernate communally in winter, sometimes in large numbers. They give birth to live young and use their keen sense of smell and tongue-flicking to locate prey. In BC, several subspecies exist, including the common western garter snake and the more aquatic valley garter snake. In the Sea-to-Sky the most frequently seen is the common garter snake
05-05-10-garter-snake.unit_of_measure: habitat suitability, composite cores and conenctivity potential
